title: Specimen, Essay and Proof: how to tell them apart
excerpt: Perforations, overprints and zero serials — the marks that separate a trial piece from a circulating note.
meta: Differences between specimen, essay and proof banknotes: perforations, overprints and zero serials explained with examples.
body:
The term **specimen** identifies examples distributed to central banks and museums as official samples, not for circulation. They are typically perforated or overprinted with the word SPECIMEN.

An **essay** is a trial piece produced before the definitive issue, often with technical differences from the final version.

A **proof** is a sheet produced during the quality control process, rarely distributed outside archives.

title: Hyperinflation compared: Zimbabwe, Weimar and Yugoslavia
excerpt: Three episodes, billions of zeros. What the highest-denomination notes ever printed really tell us.
meta: The highest-denomination banknotes in history: what the hyperinflations of Zimbabwe, Weimar and Yugoslavia tell us.
body:
Weimar Germany in 1923, Yugoslavia in 1994, Zimbabwe in 2008: three episodes that produced some of the most extraordinary banknotes in numismatic history.

**Zimbabwe** issued a 100 billion dollar note in 2008 — still insufficient to buy an egg. The monetary collapse was caused by land redistribution policies and mass money printing.

The **Weimar Reichsmark** reached a peak of 4.2 billion marks per dollar in November 1923, before stabilisation through the Rentenmark.

title: The UNCIVPOL wartime overprints of Sarajevo
excerpt: The 1993 Bosnian series — one of the most sought-after among overprint collectors.
meta: The 1993 Bosnian series with UNCIVPOL overprints: history, varieties and collector value.
body:
The 1993 Bosnian banknotes with UNCIVPOL overprint are among the most sought-after in the war numismatics market.

During the Balkans conflict, some issues were officially marked for internal UNPROFOR use. The rarest varieties show the overprint applied in red ink on pre-war specimens.

title: UNC, aUNC, VF: reading a banknote's preservation
excerpt: A practical map of the grading scale, with high-resolution front and back examples.
meta: A guide to banknote grading: what UNC, aUNC, VF mean and how to assess condition with high-resolution examples.
body:
The banknote preservation scale follows IBNS international standards:

**UNC (Uncirculated)**: never circulated, no folds, full original crispness.

**aUNC (About Uncirculated)**: minimal handling traces, light corner folds.

**VF (Very Fine)**: moderately circulated, visible folds but paper still stiff.

**F (Fine)**: evident circulation, multiple folds, soft paper.

title: Banknotes of Bhutan: the kingdom that prints the Himalaya
excerpt: Dzongs, dragons and peaks: the paper money of one of the world's most isolated countries.
meta: History and iconography of Bhutan's Ngultrum banknotes: dzongs, dragons and mountains on Himalayan paper money.
body:
Bhutan has issued banknotes in Ngultrum since 1974. Issues are characterised by iconography strongly tied to the culture and geography of the Himalayan kingdom.

**Dzongs** — fortress-monasteries that serve as administrative centres — appear frequently in depictions. The thundering dragon **Druk**, national symbol, is present on almost all series.
